text:
EARLY BLIGHT (Alternaria solani) IN TOMATO AND POTATO — Management Guide

Symptoms: Dark brown concentric "target-board" spots on older/lower
leaves first, surrounded by a yellow halo. Severe infection causes
defoliation and reduced yield. Favored by warm, humid weather with
intermittent rain.

Cultural control:
- Practice crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops (avoid tomato,
  potato, brinjal in the same field for at least 2 seasons).
- Remove and burn infected plant debris after harvest; the fungus
  survives in soil and crop residue.
- Stake/trellis plants to improve airflow and reduce le

af wetness
  duration.
- Avoid overhead irrigation in the evening; irrigate early morning so
  foliage dries during the day.

Chemical/biological control:
- Spray Mancozeb (0.25%) or Chlorothalonil at first sign of spotting,
  repeating every 10-12 days.
- For organic management, Bacillus subtilis-based biofungicides or
  copper oxychloride (0.3%) are effective preventive options.
- Begin preventive sprays at flowering stage in regions with a known
  history of early blight, rather than waiting for symptoms.

Nutrient management: Balanced potassium application improves plant
resistance; avoid

excess nitrogen, which promotes lush, susceptible
foliage.

GENERAL INTEGRATED PEST MANAGEMENT (IPM) PRINCIPLES FOR SMALLHOLDER FARMS

1. Monitor before you spray: Walk the field weekly and check the
   underside of leaves. Many pest/disease problems are caught early
   enough to manage culturally (no chemicals needed) if seen in time.

2. Economic threshold: Not every pest sighting requires treatment.
   Spraying only when pest/disease levels are likely to cause real
   yield loss saves money and protects beneficial insects.

3. Rotate chemical groups: Using the same fungicide/insecticide
   repeatedly leads to resistance. Alternate between different

chemical classes (consult the product label's mode-of-action group).

4. Border/trap crops: Planting marigold around vegetable plots or
   maize around groundnut can intercept and reduce certain pest
   pressures naturally.

5. Water management: Most fungal diseases (blight, leaf spot,
   anthracnose) are worsened by prolonged leaf wetness. Morning
   irrigation and drip systems (vs. overhead sprinkler) significantly
   reduce disease pressure.

6. Local advisory: Andhra Pradesh's Rythu Bharosa Kendras (RBKs) and
   nearby Krishi Vigyan Kendras (KVKs) issue weekly pest/disease
   advisories

specific to the district and season -- always cross-check
   any general guidance against the current local bulletin before
   large-scale spraying decisions.

GROUNDNUT TIKKA / LEAF SPOT DISEASE — Management Guide

Symptoms: Two forms common in Andhra Pradesh -- early leaf spot (light
brown circular spots) and late leaf spot (darker, near-black spots
with yellow halo), both causing premature defoliation and yield loss
of 20-50% if untreated.

Cultural control:
- Use certified disease-free seed; treat seed with Trichoderma viride
  (4g/kg seed) before sowing.
- Follow 2-3 year crop rotation with cereals (maize, sorghum) to break
  the disease cycle in soil.
- Deep summer ploughing to expose and destroy fungal spores surviving
  in crop residue.
- Tim

ely sowing (avoid late sowing) reduces disease pressure, as late-
  sown crops face spot disease during peak humidity periods.

Chemical/biological control:
- First spray of Hexaconazole (0.1%) or Chlorothalonil at 30-35 days
  after sowing, repeated at 15-day intervals up to 3-4 sprays.
- Carbendazim + Mancozeb combination products are effective against
  both early and late leaf spot when alternated to avoid resistance.
- Neem seed kernel extract (5%) as a softer alternative for low-to-
  moderate infestations.

Regional note: Chittoor district groundnut growers should coordinate
spray timin

g with the local KVK's pest/disease advisory bulletin, as
timing relative to monsoon onset significantly affects efficacy.

MANGO ANTHRACNOSE (Colletotrichum gloeosporioides) — Management Guide

Symptoms: Black/dark brown irregular spots on leaves, flowers, and
fruit. On fruit, spots enlarge and sink during ripening, leading to
post-harvest rot. Major cause of flower/fruit drop in humid weather.

Cultural control:
- Prune and destroy infected twigs and dead wood after harvest to
  reduce fungal inoculum for the next flowering season.
- Maintain adequate spacing and canopy management for airflow,
  reducing the humid micro-climate the fungus needs.
- Avoid overhead/sprinkler irrigation during flowering and fruit
  d

evelopment stages.

Chemical/biological control:
- Spray Carbendazim (0.1%) or Copper oxychloride (0.3%) at panicle
  emergence, repeating at 15-day intervals through fruit set.
- Pre-harvest spray of Mancozeb (0.25%) 15 days before harvest helps
  reduce post-harvest anthracnose development in storage/transit.
- Hot water treatment of harvested fruit (52°C for 5 minutes) reduces
  latent infection before it shows up during ripening.

Storage note: Anthracnose often stays latent in green fruit and only
appears during ripening -- so field-stage prevention matters more than
post-harvest treatmen

TOMATO LEAF CURL VIRUS (ToLCV) — Management Guide

Symptoms: Upward curling of leaves, yellowing between veins, stunted
growth, reduced fruit size. Spread primarily by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci).

Cultural control:
- Use virus-free, certified nursery seedlings.
- Remove and destroy infected plants immediately (roguing) to prevent
  spread to healthy plants.
- Avoid planting tomato near cotton, okra, or cucurbits, which host
  the same whitefly vector.
- Maintain 60x45 cm spacing for good airflow, reducing humidity that
  favors whitefly buildup.

Chemical/biological control:
- Yellow sticky tra

ps at 15-20 per acre to monitor and trap whiteflies.
- Spray neem oil (3%) or neem-based formulations at 7-10 day intervals
  as a preventive measure, especially in the first 30 days after
  transplanting.
- If whitefly population is high, use recommended systemic insecticides
  as per local Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) advisory; always follow
  label dosage and pre-harvest interval.

Resistant varieties: Several hybrid tomato varieties with ToLCV
tolerance are available through state agricultural universities;
consult your nearest KVK for region-specific recommendations.
